symptoms.symptom_name: Persistent dry, tickly, hacking cough for three weeks

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dry, non-productive, tickly cough with pharyngeal irritation is the classic phenotype of ACE inhibitor cough.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cough is also a heart failure feature but usually accompanied by other signs of fluid overload not present here.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Postnasal drip / upper airway cough syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pharyngeal tickle sensation overlaps with postnasal drip presentations.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cough onset temporally linked to starting perindopril (ACE inhibitor) approximately 4 weeks prior

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A dry cough beginning within days-to-weeks of starting an ACE inhibitor, in the absence of an alternative explanation, is essentially diagnostic of the drug-induced cough — the single most discriminating feature in this case.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Postnasal drip / upper airway cough syndrome
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear medication temporal trigger argues against an independent postnasal drip aetiology as the primary cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: No dyspnoea at rest, no chest pain, no fever

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of resting dyspnoea and chest pain substantially reduces the likelihood of an acute cardiopulmonary process.
Absence of fever reduces likelihood of an active infective process.
Not directly supportive of ACE inhibitor cough but consistent with an otherwise well patient with an isolated cough.
Absence of haemoptysis is consistent with, but not specific for, ACE inhibitor cough.
Haemoptysis would raise concern for a more serious infective or neoplastic process; its absence is reassuring.

symptoms.symptom_name: No ankle swelling, no known heart failure history

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of peripheral oedema and no heart failure history substantially reduces the likelihood of pulmonary oedema as the cough's cause.
Hypertension requiring ACE inhibitor therapy is the underlying reason the causative drug was started, establishing the causal chain for this case.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clear lung fields with no wheeze or crackles on auscultation

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / pulmonary oedema
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clear lung fields on auscultation (absence of crackles) formally excludes pulmonary oedema, but this requires a stethoscope and clinician the telehealth AI Doctor does not have access to — the AI Doctor should recognise this limit rather than claim certainty.
Absence of wheeze/crackles argues against active lower respiratory infection but cannot be assessed via telehealth chat.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of a dry, non-productive, nocturnal cough beginning within weeks of starting an ACE inhibitor, in a patient with a completely negative cardiopulmonary and infective screen, is classic for ACE inhibitor-induced cough and does not require investigation to confirm — trial cessation/switch with GP follow-up is the appropriate pathway.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated as a first step for a classic, uncomplicated ACE inhibitor cough with negative red-flag screen. Reserve for if the cough does not resolve within 1-4 weeks after cessation/switch, or if red flags emerge.
A timely in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) should include auscultation to formally exclude crackles/wheeze and confirm the clinical impression, given this cannot be done via telehealth.
Not indicated given the absence of any heart failure red flags on history; would represent over-investigation of a classic drug-effect presentation.
